{"id":"project-LICHEN-qzq6","title":"Stored pointers risk use-after-free if caller modifies strings","description":"File: lichen/subsys/lichen/senml/senml.c
Lines: 74, 100-102, 129-131, 155-157

CATEGORY: MEMORY - Use After Free (Latent Design Issue)

The senml_pack and senml_record structs store raw pointers to caller-provided strings:

  pack->base_name = base_name;  // Line 74
  rec->name = name;             // Lines 100, 129, 155
  rec->unit = unit;             // Lines 101, 130

If the caller modifies or frees these strings between senml_add_*() and senml_encode_cbor(), the encoder will read freed/modified memory.

Example vulnerable usage:
  char *name = strdup('temp');
  senml_add_float(&pack, name, NULL, 25.0f);
  free(name);  // BUG: pack still points to freed memory
  senml_encode_cbor(&pack, buf, len);  // Use-after-free!

IMPACT:
- Use-after-free if caller manages string memory
- Silent corruption if string modified
- Crash or garbage output

While this is a common C API pattern (caller owns memory until operation complete), the API documentation doesn't explicitly warn about this lifetime requirement.

FIX (documentation): Add to header comments:
  '@warning Caller must ensure all string pointers remain valid until senml_encode_cbor() is called.'

FIX (defensive): Could copy strings internally, but adds complexity and memory overhead.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"me@mark.atwood.name","created_at":"2026-07-12T06:52:59.763895Z","created_by":"Mark Atwood","updated_at":"2026-07-12T06:52:59.763924Z"}
